title: Red Wine on Carpet
timeEstimate: 10–45 minutes (wet) or up to a couple of hours (dry)
stainChemistry: Red wine is a tannin stain — anthocyanin polyphenols bound to face yarn tightly. Carpet adds a fiber-AND-construction risk: nylon / olefin / polyester face yarn is what the wine sits on, but pooled liquid sinks through to the latex backing and the pad beneath, where it wicks back up for days as the carpet dries. The visible stain on the pile is half the problem; the invisible contamination in the backing + pad is the other half. Heat accelerates tannin oxidation and can lock a reversible purple stain into a permanent brown one within 15–30 minutes. Treatment is extraction-first, chemistry-small-amounts, always.
whyThisWorks: Extraction beats chemistry on carpet. The rule is damp-cloth transfer, never pour — pooled liquid is what ruins the pad. Cold blot flushes what water can carry. NSD emulsifies what's bound. Acid-side tannin spotter disrupts the anthocyanin-fiber grip. 3% H₂O₂ oxidizes residual color. Between every wet pass, a weighted dry towel pulls moisture UP and out of the backing before it can migrate into the pad. Skip the extraction step and the stain wicks back up the next time the carpet gets damp.
defaultAssumption: Assume synthetic face yarn (nylon / olefin / polyester) unless user says wool. Wool carpet + red wine escalates earlier — wool is protein fiber, narrower chemistry lane.

spottingProtocol.technique: Press dry, lift, repeat — no rubbing
spottingProtocol.equipment: Clean white cotton towels, firm pressure
spottingProtocol.instruction: Press a clean folded white towel straight down onto the spill with firm pressure. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work from the outside of the stain inward. Never rub — rubbing drives wine deep into the pile and then into the pad. Stop when the towel stops picking up red.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD in cold water, damp white cloth, wet/dry vacuum or weighted dry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply cold NSD via a damp cloth — press onto the spot, lift, rotate cloth. Never pour on carpet. Immediately extract with a wet/dry vac or weighted dry towel on top. Repeat 2–4 cycles until the towel comes up clean. Extraction between every wet pass is the rule that protects the pad.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — acid-side tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply acid-side tannin spotter by dropper directly to the stain — never pour. The acid disrupts the anthocyanin-fiber grip. Dwell 5–10 min. Blot with dry towel. On any dyed or printed carpet, test a hidden patch first.

Apply 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop on residual color. Peroxide oxidizes the anthocyanin conjugated system. Check every 5 minutes for color break. Blot with a dry towel. One oxidizer at a time; flush with NSD-damp cloth before a second pass.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD damp cloth, weighted dry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: NSD-damp cloth to clear residual spotter and oxidizer. Then a folded DRY towel on the spot with a book or heavy pan on top — weighted 30 minutes to pull moisture UP from the backing toward the surface. Residual liquid left in the pad will wick wine color back up over the next 24 hours.

Fan the area. Air-dry fully. Inspect the next day for wicking — if a faint ring returns as the carpet dries, the pad is contaminated. Escalate to a professional carpet cleaner with extraction equipment. Pad contamination is the invisible failure mode of home carpet treatment.

steps: 1. Blot + extract dry — press, lift, repeat. Never rub.
2. Cold NSD via damp cloth, extract between every pass.
3. Acid-side tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter / PRENETT-A) by dropper. 5–10 min.
4. 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop on residual. 5–15 min, check every 5. One oxidizer at a time.
5. NSD-damp flush + weighted dry towel, 30 min, to pull moisture up from backing.
6. Fan-dry. Inspect next day for wicking — pad contamination = specialist.
products: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A)
Wet/dry vacuum or weighted dry towels
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — hot water activates backing adhesive + accelerates tannin browning
warnings: Never pour or pool liquid on carpet — sinks to backing and pad
Never use hot water — accelerates tannin browning + activates backing adhesive
Never skip extraction between wet passes — pad moisture causes wicking + mildew
One oxidizer at a time — NSD-flushed between
Wool carpet narrows the chemistry lane — escalate earlier

homeSolutions.instruction: Find a hidden patch of the same carpet — inside a closet, under a couch, behind a door. Dab a drop of cool water + Dawn on it with a white cloth. Wait 2 minutes. If the white cloth comes up with carpet dye on it, stop — your carpet's dye isn't stable and home chemistry will lighten it. Skip to step 6 (call a pro).

homeSolutions.agent: Avoid Alkaline Cleaners on Red Wine
homeSolutions.instruction: Red wine is a tannin. Alkaline products — ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean, many "all-purpose" carpet sprays — LOCK tannin into the fiber and turn a purple ring into a permanent brown one. Use ONLY: cold water, Dawn dish soap, 3% hydrogen peroxide, and (optionally) white vinegar. Salt and club soda don't help much, but they don't hurt.

Press a thick stack of white paper towels or a folded white cloth straight down on the spill. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work from the outside of the stain toward the center so it doesn't spread. Keep blotting until the cloth stops picking up red. Rubbing is the #1 mistake — it drives the wine deep into the pile and into the pad below, and once it's in the pad you can't reach it from the top.

Pour a few TABLESPOONS of cold water directly on the stain — not a cup, a few tablespoons. Blot immediately. Repeat two or three times. You want to rinse the wine off the fibers without flooding the pad. Hot water is off-limits — it sets the tannin into a permanent brown color.

Mix 1 tablespoon clear Dawn dish soap with 1 cup 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide in a small bowl. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, dab the stain — edges toward center. Let it sit 5–10 minutes, blot with a clean damp cloth. Peroxide oxidizes the wine pigment; Dawn lifts it. Most wine stains need 2–3 cycles. This is the workhorse step — give it time.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with ammonia or bleach — toxic fume risk. Skip this step on dark or patterned carpet unless your patch test was clean.

homeSolutions.agent: Weighted Dry Towel Between Every Wet Pass
homeSolutions.instruction: This is the step most home guides skip. After each wet pass, put a THICK DRY towel on the spot and weight it with a book or a heavy pan for 20–30 minutes. The dry towel pulls moisture UP and out of the carpet backing before it can soak into the pad. If you skip this, the wine color wicks back up as the carpet dries over the next 24 hours.

Point a fan at the spot. Let it dry completely before walking on it — several hours. Inspect the next day in daylight. If a faint pink ring returns as the carpet dries, the wine is in the pad and home treatment has plateaued. Call a carpet cleaner.

If the stain wicks back, if your patch test failed, if the carpet is wool, or if two full home rounds didn't clear it — call a professional carpet cleaner. They have hot-water extraction equipment that reaches the pad, which home tools can't. Tell them: "Red wine on [nylon / wool / synthetic] carpet, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide + weighted dry towel. [Ring wicked back / stain plateau'd / patch test failed]."

steps: 1. Patch-test a hidden area with Dawn + 3% peroxide first.
2. NO alkaline cleaners — ammonia/OxiClean/baking soda lock tannin in as permanent brown.
3. Blot straight down from outside to center. Never rub.
4. Cold water dilute in small amounts — tablespoons, not cups.
5. Dawn + 3% peroxide solution, dab with damp cloth, dwell 5–10 min. 2–3 cycles.
6. Weighted dry towel 20–30 min between every wet pass — this is the pad-protection step.
7. Fan-dry, don't walk on it. Inspect next day for wicking.
8. Escalate to a carpet pro if it wicks back, if wool carpet, or two home rounds failed.
products: Clean white towels or thick paper towel stack
A heavy book or pan (for weighted drying)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain wicks back after drying, if the carpet is wool, if your patch test shows dye bleed, or if two full home rounds don't clear it. Call a carpet cleaner with hot-water extraction.
warnings: NEVER use alkaline cleaners (ammonia, OxiClean, baking soda) on red wine — locks tannin as permanent brown
NEVER pour or pool liquid on carpet — sinks to backing and pad
NEVER skip the weighted dry-towel extraction between wet passes
NEVER mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach
NEVER use hot water — sets tannin brown

neverDo: Never pour or pool liquid on carpet — pad contamination
Never use alkaline cleaners on red wine — tannin locks as permanent brown
Never use hot water — sets tannin into carpet fiber
Never skip extraction between wet passes
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time, flushed between
Never walk on the spot while drying — drives moisture deeper
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / olefin / polyester face yarn: tolerates NSD, acid-side spotters, 3% H₂O₂ at room temp
Wool carpet: protein fiber — narrower chemistry, skip peroxide, escalate earlier
Backing latex: dissolves under pooled solvent or hot water — the invisible failure mode

materialWarnings: NO alkaline cleaners on tannin — locks as permanent brown
Extract moisture between every wet pass

escalation.when: Stain wicks back as carpet dries (pad contamination), wool carpet, patch test shows dye bleed, or two home rounds haven't cleared it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Red wine on [nylon / wool / synthetic] carpet, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + 3% H₂O₂ + weighted dry towel. [Ring wicked back / plateau / dye-test failed.]"
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with hot-water extraction

contextQuestions: How long ago did the spill happen?
Is the carpet fiber nylon, olefin, polyester, or wool?
Has anything alkaline (OxiClean / ammonia / baking soda) been applied?
Did anyone walk on the spot or use hot water?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Alkaline cleaner already applied = permanent brown, pro-lane or fabric replacement. Walked-on + dried = high pad-contamination risk. Wool + red wine = pro. Synthetic + caught fresh + no alkaline = good DIY odds.
